linkedin.framing: On LinkedIn, founders identify themselves with titles like 'Founder', 'Co-founder', or 'CEO' in their headline, and often post about reaching an MVP, coming out of stealth mode, or attending a summit/hackathon/demo day.

github.framing: On GitHub, early builders label repos with terms like 'hackathon', 'agentic', 'WIP', 'proof of concept', or 'side project' rather than corporate/company language — look at repo names, descriptions, and topics.

google.framing: General web search — prefer niche blogs, personal sites, hackathon/demo pages, and small local press over mainstream/major tech press.

producthunt.framing: Product Hunt launches are framed around 'makers' and 'launch day' — prefer small launches with few upvotes over popular ones.

accelerators.framing: Accelerator/incubator sites use terms like 'cohort', 'batch', 'demo day', and 'portfolio company' — prefer regional/niche programs over globally famous ones (YC, Techstars).

typical_labels: day in the life of a founder
youtube.framing: Idea-stage founders on YouTube post 'building in public' vlogs, 'day in the life of a founder' content, and short product demo videos, often as solo/indie creators long before any company site exists.

twitter.framing: Twitter/X is where idea-stage founders post short 'building in public' threads, 'shipped' announcements, and indie-hacker content — this is often the very first public trace of a startup, before a website or company page exists.

reddit.framing: Reddit founders post in niche subreddits (r/startups, r/SaaS, r/indiehackers, or niche-specific subs) asking for feedback or sharing early progress, often under a pseudonym rather than a company identity.
